1	---
2	title: Concepts
3	weight: 2
4	---
5	
6	The ideas behind DDx and document-driven development.
7	
8	## What DDx Is
9	
10	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is the **shared infrastructure
11	platform for document-driven development**. It provides the primitives that
12	developers and workflow tools use to maintain, compose, and deliver the
13	documents AI agents consume to build software.
14	
15	The core thesis: **document quality, not agent capability, is the bottleneck.**
16	Well-maintained abstractions — requirements, architecture, design, tests —
17	produce better software than working at the code level alone. DDx encodes that
18	insight into infrastructure.
19	
20	<!-- diagram: documents-drive-agents -->
21	
22	## The Three-Layer Stack
23	
24	DDx is one layer in a three-project stack with explicit boundaries. Each layer
25	is independently useful and replaceable.
26	
27	| Layer | Project | Owns |
28	|-------|---------|------|
29	| **Platform** | [DDx](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/ddx) | Document library, bead tracker, agent dispatch, personas, templates, git sync |
30	| **Workflow** | [HELIX](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/helix) | Phases, gates, supervisory dispatch, methodology |
31	| **Quality** | [Dun](https://github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/dun) | Check discovery, execution, agent-friendly output |
32	
33	DDx provides primitives. HELIX and others provide opinions. Dun verifies the
34	result.
35	
36	<!-- diagram: three-layer-stack -->
37	
38	{{< callout type="info" >}}
39	**Why three layers?** Mixing methodology into platform code locks teams into
40	one way of working. Keeping the platform opinion-free lets HELIX evolve, lets
41	alternative workflows exist, and lets DDx serve both fully-autonomous and
42	human-driven teams from the same primitives.
43	{{< /callout >}}
44	
45	## Documents Are the Product
46	
47	The fundamental shift: **you maintain documents, agents produce code.**
48	
49	In traditional development, code is the primary artifact. In document-driven
50	development, the primary artifacts are the documents that tell agents what to
51	build and how to build it:
52	
53	- **Prompts** — instructions that direct agent behavior for specific tasks
54	- **Personas** — behavioral definitions that shape how agents approach work
55	- **Patterns** — proven solutions to recurring problems, written for agent
56	  consumption
57	- **Templates** — project and file blueprints with variable substitution
58	- **Specs** — requirements and designs that define what to build
59	
60	The quality of agent output follows directly from the quality of these
61	documents. Better documents produce better code, every time.
62	
63	## What DDx Provides
64	
65	- **Document library** — structured, versioned, agent-discoverable artifacts
66	  in the repository.
67	- **Bead tracker** — work items with a dependency DAG, ready/blocked queues,
68	  and JSONL interchange. Beads are the unit of work agents execute.
69	- **Agent service** — unified harness dispatch (Claude, Codex, Gemini, local
70	  models) with quorum review, session logging, and a single prompt envelope.
71	- **Execute-loop** — drain the bead queue with isolated worktrees, automatic
72	  review, and recovery. Cheap models do, strong models review.
73	- **Project-local install** — `ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only touch
74	  `<projectRoot>`. The only global artifact is `ddx-server`.
75	- **Single `ddx` skill** — one consolidated skill, not a fleet. One surface
76	  for agents to learn.
77	
78	## What DDx Is Not
79	
80	- **Not a methodology.** No phases, no gates, no prescribed artifact types —
81	  workflow tools own those.
82	- **Not a storage system.** Files in Git. No proprietary backend.
83	- **Not an editor or IDE.** Editing happens wherever you already work.
84	- **Not opinionated about agents.** Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out
85	  contract plugs in.
86	
87	## Read Next
88	
89	- [Principles](principles/) — the load-bearing decisions behind DDx.
90	- [Architecture](architecture/) — how beads, the execute-loop, personas, and
91	  the project-local install model fit together.
92	- [Glossary](glossary/) — quick definitions for the terms used across the
93	  docs.
94

1	---
2	title: Architecture
3	weight: 3
4	---
5	
6	This page covers the concrete moving parts: how beads flow through the queue,
7	how the execute-loop runs an agent against a bead, how personas attach to
8	roles, and what the project-local install model means on disk.
9	
10	For exhaustive specifications, follow the `FEAT-*` references into
11	`docs/helix/01-frame/features/` in the repository.
12	
13	## Bead Lifecycle
14	
15	A bead is a self-contained unit of work with an ID, title, description,
16	acceptance criteria, labels, and a dependency edge set. Beads live in the
17	project's bead store and move through a small set of states.
18	
19	```
20	created → ready → in-progress → review → closed
21	              ↘ blocked ↗
22	```
23	
24	- **created** — a bead exists but isn't pickable yet (often because deps
25	  aren't closed).
26	- **ready** — all dependencies are closed; the bead is in the work queue.
27	- **in-progress** — an agent or developer has claimed it.
28	- **review** — implementation done; an automated or human review is checking
29	  the acceptance criteria.
30	- **closed** — review passed; the bead is done.
31	- **blocked** — surfaced separately when something external prevents
32	  progress.
33	
34	`ddx bead ready` lists pickable work. `ddx bead status` shows the queue
35	shape. `ddx bead dep tree` visualizes the DAG. See **FEAT-004** for the bead
36	tracker spec.
37	
38	<!-- diagram: bead-lifecycle -->
39	
40	{{< callout type="info" >}}
41	**Why beads, not tickets?** Beads are local files in JSONL, not records in
42	an external tracker. They diff, they merge, they branch with the code.
43	Importing from `bd`/`br` keeps you portable.
44	{{< /callout >}}
45	
46	## The Execute-Loop
47	
48	The execute-loop is how DDx drains the bead queue with agents. The loop
49	picks a ready bead, runs an agent against it in an isolated worktree, and
50	either merges the result or escalates.
51	
52	```
53	┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
54	│  ddx work  (alias for ddx agent execute-loop)      │
55	└────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
56	        │
57	        ▼
58	   pick ready bead ── no ready beads ──▶ exit
59	        │
60	        ▼
61	   create execution worktree from base rev
62	        │
63	        ▼
64	   run agent (cheap tier) with bead prompt
65	        │
66	        ▼
67	   review by stronger model against acceptance criteria
68	        │
69	   ┌────┴────┐
70	   pass      fail
71	   │          │
72	   ▼          ▼
73	  merge     reopen bead, escalate to higher tier
74	   │          │
75	   └────┬─────┘
76	        ▼
77	   next bead
78	```
79	
80	The loop is **cost-tiered by design**: the implementer is a cheap model, the
81	reviewer is a stronger one, and deterministic checks (Dun) sit above review
82	catching what slipped through. Failed reviews thread the findings into the
83	next attempt's prompt so the escalating model knows exactly what was missed.
84	
85	`ddx work` is the user-facing entry point. Under the hood it calls
86	`ddx agent execute-loop` with all flags passed through. See **FEAT-006**
87	(agent service) and **FEAT-010** (executions) for the runtime contract.
88	
89	<!-- diagram: execute-loop -->
90	
91	## Personas and Role Binding
92	
93	A **persona** is a document that shapes how an agent behaves. DDx ships
94	personas like `code-reviewer`, `implementer`, `test-engineer`, and
95	`architect`. Each is a Markdown file with behavioral instructions.
96	
97	A **role** is an abstract function — "the thing that reviews code", "the
98	thing that implements features". Projects bind specific personas to roles
99	in `.ddx.yml`:
100	
101	```yaml
102	persona_bindings:
103	  code-reviewer: code-reviewer
104	  architect: architect
105	  implementer: cheap-implementer
106	```
107	
108	When an agent is dispatched for a role, the bound persona is composed into
109	the prompt envelope along with bead context, project config, and any
110	relevant patterns. Swapping a persona is a one-line config change, not a
111	code change.
112	
113	This separation is why execute-loop's cost tiering works cleanly — the
114	implementer role binds to a cheap-tier persona, the reviewer role binds to
115	a strong-tier one, and the loop never hardcodes a model name.
116	
117	<!-- diagram: persona-role-binding -->
118	
119	## Project-Local Install Model
120	
121	DDx installs are **project-local**. The only global artifact is the `ddx`
122	binary itself (and optionally `ddx-server`). Everything else lives under the
123	project root.
124	
125	After `ddx init`:
126	
127	```
128	<projectRoot>/
129	├── .ddx/
130	│   ├── config.yaml          # project DDx config
131	│   ├── plugins/             # installed plugins (local only)
132	│   │   └── ddx/             # the default DDx plugin (library)
133	│   ├── executions/          # execute-loop evidence
134	│   └── beads/               # bead store (JSONL)
135	├── .agents/
136	│   └── skills/              # agent-facing skills
137	└── .claude/
138	    └── skills/              # Claude-specific skill installs
139	```
140	
141	`ddx install <plugin>` only writes under those three trees. There is no
142	`~/.ddx`. There is no `ddx install --global`. Cloning the repo gives a
143	collaborator the entire DDx surface for the project; deleting `.ddx/`
144	removes it.
145	
146	This is a deliberate inversion of the usual CLI pattern. It makes:
147	
148	- **Reproducibility** — the repo is the source of truth.
149	- **Onboarding** — `git clone && ddx doctor` is enough.
150	- **Cleanup** — reversible by file deletion.
151	
152	See **FEAT-015** for the installation architecture spec.
153	
154	<!-- diagram: project-local-install -->
155	
156	## How the Pieces Fit
157	
158	The bead tracker decides **what** to do. The persona system decides **how**
159	the agent should approach it. The execute-loop runs the agent and routes
160	results. The project-local install model means all of that is in the repo,
161	not in a developer's home directory or a remote service.
162	
163	That's the whole platform. Phases, gates, and methodology live one layer up
164	in HELIX; deterministic verification lives in Dun.
165

input.content:
# Hero graphic — blocked

The microsite landing hero (AC#3 of bead `ddx-5028c8e6`) was not generated
because `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` was not available at execution time and the
`nano-banana-pro-openrouter` skill was not installed in the runtime
environment. Per the bead's AC#3 contingency, the SVG/Mermaid diagrams
(AC#1, AC#2) shipped in this attempt and the hero portion is deferred with
this blocker note + a ready integration point.

## To finish the hero in a follow-up

1. Set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in your shell (or this project's `.env`).
2. Install the skill: `ddx install nano-banana-pro-openrouter` (or invoke
   it via the Claude skill harness if running inside Claude Code).
3. Use the prompt below to generate variants (≥ 2K, pick the strongest).
4. Save the chosen image at `website/static/hero/landing.webp` (preferred
   for size) or `website/static/hero/landing.png`.
5. In `website/content/_index.md`, uncomment the
   `<!-- HERO_GRAPHIC_PLACEHOLDER ... -->` block (search for that marker).
6. Run `cd website && hugo --minify` to verify the build, then commit.

## Generation prompt

> A distinctive, editorial-style illustration of document-driven
> development: a structured tree of documents — specs, prompts, personas,
> patterns, templates rendered as crisp paper cards with subtle markdown
> typography — flowing along clean directional channels into a small
> cluster of agent figures (abstract geometric, not anthropomorphic
> robots), which in turn emit code, tests, and build artifacts as a
> secondary stream. Composition: documents-on-the-left, agents-in-the-
> middle, artifacts-on-the-right; strong horizontal flow lines suggest
> "documents drive the agents."
>
> Style: precise vector-feel illustration with soft gradients, muted
> editorial palette (deep indigo, warm parchment, signal teal accents,
> single warm-orange highlight on the active document), subtle paper
> grain. Avoid: glowing neural-net spheres, glowing circuit boards,
> humanoid robots, generic "AI brain" iconography, neon cyan/magenta
> gradients, glassmorphism, generic SaaS-hero crowd shots. The image
> should read as a calm engineering diagram crossed with a New Yorker
> spot illustration — distinctive, not generic AI.
>
> Aspect ratio: 16:9 (or 21:9 for wide hero). Resolution: ≥ 2048 px on
> the long edge. No text, no watermark.

## Why the contingency, not a stub

The bead AC explicitly allows AC#3 to be replaced by a documented blocker
+ a ready integration point when the key/skill are unavailable. Shipping a
placeholder PNG or generic stock art would violate AC#5 ("no generic AI
clip art").

data.text:
Committed `36bac94e`. AC walkthrough:

1. **Diagrams for the 5 named topics** — Mermaid blocks for `three-layer-stack`, `bead-lifecycle`, `execute-loop`, `persona-binding`, `project-local-install` (plus a bonus `documents-drive-agents`) in `concepts/_index.md` and `concepts/architecture.md`.
2. **V2 placeholders filled** — all six `<!-- diagram: ... -->` markers replaced.
3. **Hero (AC#3 contingency)** — `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` unset and skill not installed; deferred per the bead's contingency. `website/static/hero/HERO_BLOCKER.md` carries the prompt + integration steps; `_index.md` has a `HERO_GRAPHIC_PLACEHOLDER` block ready to uncomment.
4. **Hugo build** — `hugo --minify` exits 0 (143 pages, 0 errors); Mermaid present in rendered HTML.
5. **No generic AI clip art** — none shipped; the deferred prompt explicitly excludes it.
